title: Ancient Octopus Giants: 20-Meter-Long Apex Predators of the Cretaceous
summary:
Imagine an octopus as long as a school bus — that's what scientists have discovered about the ancient relatives of today's clever, squishy octopuses. A new study published in *Science* on April 23, 2026, led by researchers at Hokkaido University, reveals that the earliest octopuses were not shy, elusive creatures but massive predators that hunted at the very top of the marine food chain.

Tracing octopus evolution is tricky because their soft bodies rarely fossilize. So the team focused on fossilized jaws — the hard parts most likely to survive. Using high-resolution grinding tomography and an AI model, they examined jaws embedded in rock from the Late Cretaceous period (100 to 72 million years ago), collected from sites in Japan and Vancouver Island.

The jaws belonged to an extinct group called Cirrata — finned octopuses. By studying the size, shape, and wear patterns, researchers determined these animals were active hunters that could crush hard prey with powerful bites. "Our findings suggest that the earliest octopuses were gigantic predators that occupied the top of the marine food chain in the Cretaceous," says Professor Yasuhiro Iba. Some reached lengths of nearly 20 meters — possibly larger than many marine reptiles of the time.

The most surprising clue? Extensive wear on the jaws: chipping, scratching, cracking, and polishing. In some specimens, up to 10% of the jaw tip had worn away — more than in modern octopuses that eat hard-shelled prey. This indicates repeated, forceful biting. Uneven wear on one side suggests lateralization (favoring one side), which in modern animals is linked to advanced brain function. So these ancient octopuses might have been intelligent, too.

This discovery pushes back the earliest known record of finned octopuses by 15 million years and the broader octopus timeline by 5 million years, placing their origins around 100 million years ago. It also challenges the long-held view that ancient marine ecosystems were dominated only by vertebrate predators (animals with backbones). "This study provides the first direct evidence that invertebrates could evolve into giant, intelligent apex predators," says Iba. The combination of powerful jaws and loss of a hard skeleton — traits shared with marine vertebrates — may have been key to becoming huge, smart marine predators.

The study also highlights how AI can help uncover hidden fossils and reconstruct ancient ecosystems in greater detail. So next time you see a small octopus at the aquarium, remember: its great-great-great-grandparent was a 20-meter terror of the deep!
why_it_matters: This discovery upends the common belief that only animals with backbones, like dinosaurs and marine reptiles, could be top predators—showing that evolution can produce clever, enormous hunters from very different body plans, and it opens up new ways to use AI to find fossils that were previously invisible.

keywords.explanation: Animals at the top of the food chain with no natural enemies.
To turn into a fossil, preserving remains in rock.
A technique that creates 3D images by taking many X‑ray slices.
A time period from 145 to 66 million years ago when dinosaurs lived.
An extinct group of finned octopuses from the Cretaceous.
Preferring one side of the body, linked to brain specialization.

options: That they were dominated only by vertebrate predators
That all marine predators had backbones
That invertebrates could not grow large
That the Cretaceous ocean had no apex predators
questions.correct_answer: That they were dominated only by vertebrate predators

questions.question: According to the article, what new tool helped uncover hidden fossils?

background_read: The Cretaceous period (145–66 million years ago) saw the rise of many marine reptiles like mosasaurs and plesiosaurs, but octopuses—soft‑bodied mollusks—were not thought to be top predators.

Octopuses today are known for their intelligence, but their evolutionary history is poorly understood because soft tissues rarely fossilize. This study from Hokkaido University (a Japanese research institution) used advanced imaging and AI to overcome that problem.

Until now, the oldest finned octopus fossils dated to about 85 million years ago. The new findings push that back to 100 million years, placing octopus origins in the middle Cretaceous, and show that even invertebrates could become huge, smart apex predators.
Article_Structure: LEAD: compares ancient octopuses to a school bus, grabs attention with size
└─ CHALLENGE: soft bodies rarely fossilize, so scientists studied jaws
METHOD: high-resolution grinding tomography + AI model, fossils from Japan & Vancouver Island
KEY FINDING: extinct Cirrata group reached nearly 20 meters, were apex predators
└─ DETAIL: jaw wear (chipping, scratching, lateralization) suggests intelligence
SIGNIFICANCE: pushes octopus timeline back 5–15 million years; challenges idea that only vertebrates dominated
└─ QUOTE: Prof. Iba on invertebrates as giant, intelligent apex predators
CONCLUSION: AI helps uncover hidden fossils; reminds reader of octopus’s giant ancestors

perspectives.description: Scientists gain a clearer picture of octopus evolution and the complexity of ancient food webs. AI and tomography demonstrate how technology can uncover fossils that were too delicate or small to spot by eye.

The evidence comes only from jaws, not whole bodies. The reconstruction of size and behavior is partly inferential, and small sample sizes from only two regions may not represent the full diversity of ancient octopuses.

The study fits a broader pattern of rethinking ancient ecosystems: many groups we think of as 'modern' actually evolved earlier and in more dramatic forms. It also highlights how convergent evolution (similar traits in unrelated animals) can produce giant predators.

If AI can identify fossil jaws inside rocks, it may help find evidence of other soft‑bodied animals from the Cretaceous and even older periods, potentially rewriting what we know about when intelligence and large size first appeared in invertebrates.
